questions.question: Explain the structure of an Oozie workflow with control nodes and action nodes.
keyPoints: Workflow is a DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) defined in XML with a start node and end/kill termination nodes.
Control nodes: start (entry), end (success), kill (failure), decision (conditional branching), fork (parallel split), join (parallel merge).
Action nodes: MapReduce, Pig, Hive, Sqoop, Shell, Java, Spark - execute actual Hadoop jobs.
Each action has 'ok' and 'error' transitions to route to next node or error handling.

questions.question: Describe Oozie Coordinator with examples of time-based and data-availability scheduling. Explain how it is used in IoT applications.
keyPoints: Coordinator schedules and triggers workflow jobs automatically based on time or data events.
Time-based: Uses frequency parameter (hours, days, months) like cron. Example: process sensor data every hour with frequency='${coord:hours(1)}'.
Data-based: Uses dataset definitions and input-events; waits for data availability before triggering. Example: waits for all hourly sensor CSV files before starting batch analysis.
Coordinator XML includes start/end times, timezone, frequency, and action with workflow reference.
IoT example - Hourly anomaly detection: Coordinator checks for new sensor logs every hour and triggers analysis workflow.
IoT example - Daily aggregation: Waits for complete day's sensor data files before processing daily statistics.
Benefits: Automation, reduced manual intervention, handles late data, manages multiple workflow instances.

questions.question: Explain the fork and join nodes in Oozie with an IoT data processing example.
keyPoints: Fork node splits workflow into multiple parallel execution paths for independent tasks.
Join node waits for all parallel paths to complete before continuing to the next action.
IoT example: After ingesting sensor data, fork into three parallel Pig jobs (clean temperature, pressure, humidity), join when all complete, then run Hive analysis.
Benefit: Reduces total processing time by running independent tasks simultaneously instead of sequentially.

questions.question: Compare Oozie Workflow, Coordinator, and Bundle. Explain with an example of a complete IoT data processing pipeline.
keyPoints: Workflow: A single DAG of actions - e.g., ingest -> clean -> analyze -> export pipeline for one batch of data.
Coordinator: Schedules multiple workflow instances - e.g., triggers the pipeline every hour or when new data arrives.
Bundle: Groups related coordinators - e.g., bundles hourly-sensor-processing, daily-aggregation, and weekly-report coordinators together.

IoT pipeline example: Bundle manages three coordinators: (1) hourly coordinator triggers ingestion workflow, (2) daily coordinator triggers aggregation workflow after all 24 hourly runs complete, (3) weekly coordinator triggers report generation.
Lifecycle management: Bundle allows starting, stopping, suspending all related pipelines as a single unit.
Benefits: Separation of concerns, reusable workflows, automated scheduling, coordinated management of complex multi-stage IoT data pipelines.
